did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and validated SkinFLNet, a deep convolutional neural network (DCNN) system utilizing model fusion and lifelong learning technologies to classify skin cancer (benign nevus, seborrheic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and malignant melanoma) from clinical images. The AI model was trained on 1215 images and evaluated on 463 images, achieving an overall classification accuracy of 85%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work developing and validating a novel deep convolutional neural network system (SkinFLNet) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods including model fusion and lifelong learning, reports results from training on 1215 images and testing on 463 images, and compares performance metrics against board-certified dermatologists. The study includes a dataset description, performance tables, and a conclusion based on new empirical findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep convolutional neural network with fusion strategy for skin cancer recognition: model development and validation
brief_summary: This study developed SkinFLNet, a deep convolutional neural network system utilizing model fusion and lifelong learning technologies to classify five skin cancer types from clinical images. The model was trained on 1215 images and validated on 463 images collected from Taichung and Taipei Veterans General Hospitals between 2015 and 2021. SkinFLNet achieved an overall classification accuracy of 85%, precision of 85%, recall of 82%, and specificity of 93%, outperforming individual DCNN models. The system's performance was comparable to, or better than, that of three board-certified dermatologists in blind evaluations. The findings suggest that SkinFLNet can effectively classify skin tumors using a relatively small dataset, potentially improving screening accuracy in clinical practice.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five categories in the dataset; 145 original images used for training, 12 for testing.
One of the five categories in the dataset; 200 original images used for training, 74 for testing.
One of the five categories in the dataset; 120 original images used for training, 26 for testing.
One of the five categories in the dataset; 350 original images used for training, 64 for testing.
One of the five categories in the dataset; 400 original images used for training, 287 for testing.

all_task_types.target: Five discrete skin tumor categories: malignant melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis, and benign nevus.
all_task_types.details: The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for skin cancer recognition. It is a multiclass classification task (5 classes) using clinical images. The system outputs probabilities for different skin tumors to determine the most likely diagnosis. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F-score, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Taichung and Taipei Veterans General Hospital Clinical Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private hospital dataset comprising digital clinical images of skin tumors diagnosed at Taichung Veterans General Hospital and Taipei Veterans General Hospital between 2015 and 2020. Images were extracted from the hospital database, taken with digital cameras (at least 8 million pixels, macro lens, macro ring flash), and diagnoses were based on pathological examination. Dermoscopic images were excluded. The study population was Fitzpatrick skin type III-IV in Taiwan.

use.training: Used to train the seven individual DCNN models and the initial SkinFLNet fusion model. 1215 images were used for training after augmentation to address class imbalance.
use.validation: 400 images were allocated for validation during the training process.
use.testing: 463 clinical images from 270 patients who visited the dermatologic outpatient department at Taichung Veterans General Hospital between January and December 2021 were used to evaluate the performance of the trained DCNN models and SkinFLNet.
use.other: A subset of 240 images (80 MBS, 80 Nev, 80 SK) was used for lifelong learning continuous training, comprising 50% from the original dataset and 50% unseen data. A test dataset of 48 images (10 MBS, 15 Nev, 13 SK) was used to evaluate performance after lifelong learning. Another subset of 275 images (68 BCC, 12 Mel, 25 SCC, 48 SK, 122 Nev) was randomly selected from the testing dataset for blind comparison with three board-certified dermatologists.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate precision, recall, F-score, sensitivity, and specificity by weighting them according to class probability to address class imbalance.
To evaluate and visualize the performance of the SkinFLNet model across different skin tumor classes.
To compare the diagnostic performance of the SkinFLNet model against human experts using a randomly selected subset of the testing dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between SkinFLNet and dermatologists were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, etc.) and visual inspection of ROC curves, without statistical significance testing.

Taichung and Taipei Veterans General Hos.description: 463 clinical images from 270 patients used to evaluate the performance of trained DCNN models and SkinFLNet.
Taichung and Taipei Veterans General Hos.notes: SkinFLNet achieved an overall classification accuracy of 85%, precision of 85%, recall of 82%, F-score of 82%, sensitivity of 82%, and specificity of 93%. Its performance was comparable to, or even better than, that of three board-certified dermatologists.

Lifelong Learning Evaluation Subset.description: A test dataset of 48 images (10 MBS, 15 Nev, 13 SK) used to evaluate performance after lifelong learning. Training subset consisted of 240 images (80 MBS, 80 Nev, 80 SK).
Lifelong Learning Evaluation Subset.notes: The lifelong learning algorithm achieved WP, WR, WF, WS, and WSP scores of 0.89, 0.90, 0.90, 0.90, and 0.92, respectively, for MBS, SK, and Nevus classes.

Dermatologist Comparison Subset.description: A randomly selected subset of 275 images (68 BCC, 12 Mel, 25 SCC, 48 SK, 122 Nev) from the testing dataset used for blind comparison with three board-certified dermatologists.

Three Board-Certified Dermatologists.performance_comparison: Comparable to, or even better than, SkinFLNet

Dermatologist Comparison Subset.notes: The SkinFLNet fusion model (InceptionV3 & ResNet50) was compared against three board-certified dermatologists. The results demonstrated that SkinFLNet's performance is comparable to, or even better than, that of board-certified dermatologists.

architecture_used.main_model: SkinFLNet (a deep convolutional neural network system utilizing model fusion and lifelong learning technologies)

architecture_details.fusion_strategy: Model fusion combining classification outcomes from paired models to identify the optimal result. The best performing fusion model used InceptionV3 and ResNet50.
architecture_details.lifelong_learning: Algorithm based on transfer learning where images with classification errors are used as input for transfer learning, retraining the model with a combined dataset of newly collected data and part of the original data.

training_details.dataset_size: 1215 images for training, 400 for validation, 463 for testing
training_details.augmentation: Augmentation applied to Mel (55 images), BCC (200 images), SCC (280 images), and SK (50 images) to address class imbalance.

preprocessing_techniques: Image augmentation applied to address class imbalance: Melanoma (55 images), Basal cell carcinoma (200 images), Squamous cell carcinoma (280 images), and Seborrheic keratosis (50 images).
Exclusion of dermoscopic images; only digital clinical images taken with cameras (at least 8 million pixels, macro lens, macro ring flash) were used.
Division of images by patient to prevent images from the same patient from being used in both training and testing sets.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to address class imbalance in the training dataset. Specific counts of augmented images per class: Melanoma (55 images), Basal cell carcinoma (200 images), Squamous cell carcinoma (280 images), and Seborrheic keratosis (50 images). Benign nevus had 0 augmented images. The paper does not specify the geometric or color transform techniques used for this augmentation.

conclusions: SkinFLNet, a deep convolutional neural network utilizing model fusion and lifelong learning technologies, achieved an overall classification accuracy of 85%, precision of 85%, recall of 82%, F-score of 82%, sensitivity of 82%, and specificity of 93% on a testing set of 463 clinical images.
SkinFLNet outperformed individual deep convolutional neural network models (ResNet50, InceptionResNetV2, InceptionV3, VGG16, VGG19, MobileNet, MobileNetV2) in skin cancer classification.
The performance of SkinFLNet was comparable to, or even better than, that of three board-certified dermatologists in blind evaluations.

Lifelong learning improved SkinFLNet's performance, increasing weight precision from 0.81 to 0.89, weight recall from 0.75 to 0.90, weight F-score from 0.78 to 0.90, weight sensitivity from 0.75 to 0.90, and weight specificity from 0.85 to 0.92.

The study demonstrates that a DCNN can perform as well as dermatologists in skin cancer classification using a relatively small dataset (less than 2000 images) of Fitzpatrick skin type III-IV populations in Taiwan.
The model can be trained on a relatively small dataset and potentially improve skin cancer screening accuracy in clinical practice.

Limitations acknowledged include the restricted generalization due to the limited number of clinical images, insufficient melanoma cases due to low incidence among Asians, and frequent misclassification of seborrheic keratosis due to diverse clinical presentations.
The authors note that relying solely on clinical images has limitations and suggest incorporating demographic covariates such as age, sex, and lesion location to enhance performance.
A biopsy and histological examination remain the gold standard for confirmation of the algorithm's diagnosis.
SkinFLNet has been successfully implemented at Taichung Veterans General Hospital in Taichung, Taiwan.
important_remarks: The study population is restricted to Fitzpatrick skin types III-IV in Taiwan, limiting generalizability to darker skin types (Fitzpatrick V-VI) which are underrepresented in current skin disease datasets.
The dataset is small (1215 training images), which may restrict the model's generalization to wider populations.
There is a significant class imbalance, particularly for malignant melanoma (only 145 original images, 12 in testing), attributed to the low incidence of melanoma among Asians.

Seborrheic keratosis (SK) was frequently misclassified as malignant or other benign conditions due to its diverse clinical presentations, highlighting limitations of relying solely on clinical images.
The model was trained and tested exclusively on digital clinical images; dermoscopic images were explicitly excluded, which differs from standard dermatological practice.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for the comparison between SkinFLNet and dermatologists; comparisons were based on descriptive metrics.
The comparison with dermatologists involved only three board-certified experts, which may not represent the broader dermatological community's performance.
The study acknowledges that biopsy and histological examination remain the gold standard for confirmation, and the AI system is intended to assist rather than replace this process.

The model has been implemented in a real-world clinical setting (Taichung Veterans General Hospital) using lifelong learning to update with new data, addressing the issue of fixed parameters in standard deep learning models.
